title: Beeswax container candle -- vanilla
subtitle: A pure beeswax jar candle with vanilla oleoresin, naturally honey-golden and clean-burning
excerpt: Beeswax burns cleaner and longer than soy wax and has a natural honey scent of its own. Vanilla oleoresin adds a warm vanilla depth to the honey base. The wax requires a higher pour temperature than soy and a larger wick.
sourceNotes: Beeswax burn characteristics vs soy wax: candle supply industry reference. Beeswax higher melt point and wick sizing: standard candlemaking reference.

recipe.batchNotes: Scale proportionally. Beeswax is denser than soy wax -- 140 g fills a 200 ml jar to about 75%.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cure for 24 hours before first burn.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Beeswax candles last for five years or more at room temperature. Bloom (a faint white film on the surface) is natural and does not affect burn quality -- buff off with a soft cloth if aesthetics matter.

glossaryTerms.definition: Rendered bee wax in pellet form. Melt point around 62 to 65°C -- significantly higher than soy wax. Burns clean, bright, and slowly. Has a natural honey scent. Requires a larger wick than soy wax for the same jar diameter.

content.text: Beeswax is the premium candle material: it burns longer and cleaner than soy wax, emits a very faint honey scent even unscented, and sets to a golden, opaque finish. The higher melt point (62 to 65°C) means
need a hotter pour and a larger wick than soy wax for the same jar size. Vanilla oleoresin complements the natural honey note without overpowering it.

items.prepNote: about 3.5% of wax weight; measure by weight -- it is very viscous
use CD-14 or CD-18 -- beeswax requires a larger wick than soy for the same jar

content.text: Warm the jar briefly in a low oven (60°C) for 5 minutes -- pouring beeswax into a cold jar causes cracking. Stick the wick tab to the centre of the warm jar base and centre the wick with the bar.

Melt 140 g of beeswax pellets in the pouring pitcher over a double boiler. Beeswax has a higher melt point than soy wax -- allow extra time. The melted beeswax will be clear and golden, not creamy-white. Monitor the temperature and remove from the heat at 75 to 80°C.

Cool the wax to 65 to 70°C, then add 5 g of vanilla oleoresin and stir until fully incorporated -- the oleoresin is very thick and needs thorough stirring. Pour into the pre-warmed jar immediately at 65 to 68°C. Beeswax sets with a harder, more even surface than soy and is less likely to develop sinkholes.
Leave undisturbed for at least two hours. Trim the wick to 5 mm before the first burn. A 24-hour cure is sufficient for beeswax.

Beeswax burns hotter and slower than soy wax. Allow the full first burn to reach the jar edges before extinguishing. Beeswax candles may develop a white 'bloom' on the surface between burns -- this is natural and does not indicate a quality problem. Buff with a soft cloth to remove.
